elements.sent-2411-001: Digital twin technology, traditionally used in industry, is increasingly recognized for its potential to enhance educational experiences.
elements.sent-2411-002: This study investigates the application of industrial digital twins (DTs) in education, focusing on how DT models of varying fidelity can support different stages of Bloom’s taxonomy in the cognitive domain.
elements.sent-2411-003: We align Bloom’s six cognitive stages with educational levels: undergraduate studies for "Remember" and "Understand," master’s level for "Apply" and "Analyze," and doctoral level for "Evaluate" and "Create." Low-fidelity DTs aid essential knowledge acquisition and skill training, providing a low-risk environment for grasping fundamental concepts.
elements.sent-2411-004: Medium-fidelity DTs offer more detailed and dynamic simulations, enhancing application skills and problem-solving.
elements.sent-2411-005: High-fidelity DTs support advanced learners by replicating physical phenomena, allowing for innovative design and complex experiments.
elements.sent-2411-006: Within this framework, large language models (LLMs) serve as mentors, assessing progress, filling knowledge gaps, and assisting with DT interactions, parameter setting, and debugging.
elements.sent-2411-007: We evaluate the educational impact using the Kirkpatrick Model, examining how each DT model’s fidelity influences learning outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-008: This framework helps educators make informed decisions on integrating DTs and LLMs to meet specific learning objectives.
elements.sent-2411-009: Due to the demand for advanced monitoring, diagnosis, and prediction within industrial systems and the development driven by Industry 4.0, using digital twins to integrate data from various sensors and IoT devices can achieve highly complex real-time simulations.
elements.sent-2411-010: These simulations reflect the physical systems and predictive capabilities that enable industrial system optimization, fault detection, and lifecycle management.
elements.sent-2411-011: Coupled with the development of machine learning technology in the field of artificial intelligence, the capabilities of digital twins have been further enhanced, allowing dynamic adaptation and improving the accuracy of predicting system behavior under different conditions, and its application in manufacturing, aerospace, energy, and other industries has increased efficiency, reliability and cost-effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-012: Given the proven benefits of digital twin technology in industry, its potential in industrial education to solve human resource development issues is becoming increasingly apparent.
elements.sent-2411-013: Digital twins can serve as powerful experiential learning tools in education to bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical application.
elements.sent-2411-014: This potential is particularly relevant when viewed through Bloom's taxonomy \cite{krathwohl2002revision .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Bloom's taxonomy is a framework that divides educational goals into levels, from lower-order cognitive skills, such as memory and comprehension, to higher-order skills, such as analysis, evaluation, and creation.
elements.sent-2411-016: This study aligns the different fidelity levels of the digital twin with the different educational stages of Bloom's taxonomy, providing a new approach to curriculum design in engineering education for different needs.
elements.sent-2411-017: That is to say, the complexity and fidelity of the digital twin model can be tailored to the cognitive needs of the educational stage.
elements.sent-2411-018: At the undergraduate level, where the focus is on acquiring fundamental knowledge and basic skills, low-fidelity digital twins can provide simplified representations of industrial systems.
elements.sent-2411-019: While low-fidelity digital twins cannot simulate precise system behavior, these models are adequate for tasks involving the core principles of "remembering" and "understanding." Low-fidelity digital twins can enable students to engage in fundamental aspects of system operation without being overwhelmed by the complexity of high-fidelity simulations, causing additional learning confusion.
elements.sent-2411-020: Features of these models may include basic manufacturing processes, simplified machine operations, or basic control systems that are sufficient for introductory courses in engineering and related disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-021: As students advance to the postgraduate level, especially master's programs, the educational focus shifts to "applied" and "analytical" knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-022: At the master's level, medium-fidelity digital twins become more appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-023: Enable students to engage in complex problem-solving activities and scenario analysis by providing more detail and interactivity.
elements.sent-2411-024: The ability to manipulate variables, test hypotheses, and analyze results in a controlled digital environment is consistent with the cognitive processes of application and analysis described in Bloom's Taxonomy.
elements.sent-2411-025: Medium-fidelity digital twins can simulate manufacturing processes with detailed parameters that students can adjust to optimize output or resolve potential issues, promoting a deeper understanding of system dynamics and interdependencies.
elements.sent-2411-026: At the doctoral level, the educational focus is on "creating" and "evaluating" new knowledge, requiring the highest degree of cognitive engagement.
elements.sent-2411-027: High-fidelity digital twins provide the detailed, accurate, and dynamic simulations required for advanced research and innovation at the doctoral level.
elements.sent-2411-028: These models enable doctoral students to design and test new systems, conduct complex experiments, and evaluate the feasibility of novel solutions in virtual environments that closely reflect real-world conditions.
elements.sent-2411-029: The integration of real-time data, advanced physics-based modeling, and artificial intelligence-driven analytics in a high-fidelity digital twin supports the rigorous requirements of doctoral research, allowing for the exploration of cutting-edge technologies and the development of new methods.
elements.sent-2411-030: Introducing digital twins into education will profoundly impact teaching and learning.
elements.sent-2411-031: From a technology perspective, using digital twins to simulate complex industrial systems in a classroom setting can provide students with real-world experience without the costs and risks associated with operating physical equipment and personal safety.
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, the scalability of digital twin technology enables educational institutions to provide customized learning experiences that adapt to the needs of individual students, thereby supporting differentiated instruction and creating more inclusive learning environments.
elements.sent-2411-033: Even at the same level of education, disadvantaged students can make up for deficiencies in their knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-034: The framework proposed in this study combines the levels of digital twin fidelity with the cognitive demands of different educational levels to enable educators to create more effective learning experiences for conveying theoretical knowledge and developing the skills needed to succeed in industry.
elements.sent-2411-035: As digital twin technology continues to develop, its role in education will likely expand, providing new opportunities to improve the quality and relevance of engineering and technology education.
elements.sent-2411-036: Digital twin technology originated from aerospace engineering and was proposed by NASA to enhance the design, monitoring, and maintenance of complex systems for space vehicles in 2010 \cite{glaessgen2012digital .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The concept of digital twins is system engineering based on simulation.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, what is different from the offline simulation is that in addition to the physical model, it also includes online sensor updates and historical data as input to the model to mirror the ultra-realistic corresponding physical system behavior \cite{shafto2010draft .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Moreover, in response to the need for universality, modern digital twins can reflect physical behavior, and user-friendly visualization is an integral part of them.
elements.sent-2411-040: Constructing the appearance of a physical system and transforming it into an intuitive and operable interface is becoming a core challenge in the development of digital twin technology \cite{tao2022digital .}
elements.sent-2411-041: We illustrate the concept of modern digital twins in Figure \ref{fig:DT .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Actual equipment operates in physical space and uses sensors to collect data on physical behavior to provide continuous information input to the virtual space.
elements.sent-2411-043: In virtual space, the digital twins are built with varying fidelity levels to mirror the system accurately at different levels, process the data obtained from the physical space, and generate behavior output or feedback control to send it back to equipment in physical space, enabling real-time adjustments and optimization.
elements.sent-2411-044: The interoperability of these two spaces can be seen as the coupling of digital twins with physical systems and is metaphorically explained by the components of the human body: the ``bone'' represents the physical system; the ``muscle'' symbolizes the behavior model of Digital Twin, responsible for simulating dynamic interactions and driving the physical system; and the ``skin'' serves as the visualization layer, which translates complex data into user-friendly, intuitive interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-045: The seamless coupling of these elements ensures the interoperability of digital twins with physical systems, enhancing monitoring, predictive maintenance, and operational efficiency across various applications.
elements.sent-2411-046: Modeling physical behavior to establish accurate virtual models and building the appearance of a physical system to transform it into an intuitive and operable interface are becoming core challenges in developing digital twin technology.
elements.sent-2411-047: In constructing a behavioral model of digital twins, it is not easy to rely on traditional numerical models to model complex systems, but with the mature development of machine learning technology and computing power, using machine learning technology to model physical behavior has become the primary option \cite{rathore2021role, min2019machine .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Machine learning can reduce the complexity of digital twin modeling, and this advantage allows digital twin technology to play a role in various fields \cite{tao2018digital .}

elements.sent-2411-049: In industrial education, the complexity and cost of industrial environments cause the challenge of workforce training.
elements.sent-2411-050: Using digital twins for industrial education to respond to workforce development needs has become a potential option and has attracted attention \cite{hazrat2023developing .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Digital twins can be used as a training tool, allowing learners to conduct ultra-realistic personalized training through the virtual and real combination of digital twins and factory environments, and can greatly improve resource optimization and operational efficiency \cite{lu2020digital .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Lei et al. proposed a web-based digital twin training environment for communication maintenance and power systems training\cite{lei2023web .}
elements.sent-2411-053: They used optical fiber fault repair as an example to teach students how to understand the actual before and after repair situations through the digital twin.
elements.sent-2411-054: Moreover, Martínez-Gutiérrez et al. proposed in 2023 the use of virtual reality combined with digital twins to train operators to operate mobile robots \cite{martinez2023convergence .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The results explained that digital twins can be combined with actual equipment to become a compromise between cost and teaching effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-056: In process engineering experimental education, Boettcher et al. implemented a jet pump in virtual reality through digital twin technology, allowing students to solve problems independently in complex and sometimes vague scenarios \cite{boettcher2023developing .}
elements.sent-2411-057: These examples illustrate the potential for digital twins in education and workforce development across different industrial sectors.
elements.sent-2411-058: However, current research on digital twins in industrial education still lacks systematic evaluation methods and potential value to higher education.
elements.sent-2411-059: This study introduces Bloom's Taxonomy into the digital learning framework to systematically evaluate learning performance.
elements.sent-2411-060: In terms of industrial education, Awouda et al. used this method to evaluate the effectiveness of open-source IoT in education \cite{awouda2024bloom .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Additionally, Gajek et al. used this method to evaluate the learning effectiveness of process safety \cite{gajek2022process .}
elements.sent-2411-062: These examples have educational objectives related to Industry 4.0 and illustrate that Bloom's Taxonomy is a suitable option for evaluating the effectiveness of industrial education.
elements.sent-2411-063: This educational framework is based on the Multi-Fidelity Digital Twin and combines different educational levels, Bloom's Taxonomy, and the Kirkpatrick models, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:framework .}
elements.sent-2411-064: These four elements are intertwined to present a comprehensive learning and assessment approach that uses digital twins to meet industrial education goals and provides an educational process from primary understanding to advanced creation while also considering learning outcomes and behavior change.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this framework, the six levels of the cognitive domain of Bloom's Taxonomy are Remembe, Understanding, Application, Analysis, Evaluate, and Create, which correspond to different stages of education -- Undergraduate, Master, and Doctoral degrees.
elements.sent-2411-066: As learners progress through the educational stage, their learning goals shift from memorizing and understanding basic knowledge to higher-level like analysis, evaluation, and creation.
elements.sent-2411-067: In the educational needs of different stages, multi-fidelity digital twins can become an educational tool, providing reasonable learning assistance for different educational levels and making educational goals at different stages successful.
elements.sent-2411-068: The application of multi-fidelity digital twin technology in education closely relates to the educational goals mentioned above.
elements.sent-2411-069: Low-Fidelity Digital Twins are usually constructed from simple 3D models and animations.
elements.sent-2411-070: They are suitable for use in the early stages of education to help students understand basic concepts and arouse interest in learning.
elements.sent-2411-071: When undergraduate students enter the workplace or graduate school, they will have the basic knowledge to meet their employment or research needs.
elements.sent-2411-072: In the stage of the Master's degree, the Medium-Fidelity Digital Twin can provide virtual commissioning, behavioral simulations, and rule-based automation that help students apply and analyze their learning.
elements.sent-2411-073: At the highest level of education, the doctoral degree, High-Fidelity Digital Twin, covers real-time interaction, machine learning predictive analysis, multi-model interoperability, etc. These technologies support creative thinking and in-depth evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-074: In evaluating students' learning effectiveness, we introduce the Kirkpatrick model \cite{smidt2009kirkpatrick into the framework, which can ensure a comprehensive evaluation of students' learning effectiveness at different educational levels.}
elements.sent-2411-075: At the undergraduate level, using low-fidelity digital twins, the emphasis is on the first level: reaction, focusing on how students perceive the training, its relevance to their future careers, and their participation in the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-076: As students advance to the Master's degree, Level 2: Learning becomes the focus, assessing how students acquire expected knowledge, skills, and attitudes through mid-fidelity digital twins, thereby facilitating deeper application and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-077: At the doctoral level, using high-fidelity digital twins, the framework moves to Level 3: Behaviors and Level 4: Result.
elements.sent-2411-078: The focus here is on how students apply their knowledge and skills in real-world settings to drive organizational results and innovation.
elements.sent-2411-079: This integration can help educators gain a comprehensive view of learner progress.

elements.sent-2411-080: Integrating LLMs as a virtual tutor to evaluate the Kirkpatrick model can provide dynamic and adaptive feedback to enhance the educational framework further.
elements.sent-2411-081: LLMs can analyze student learning responses and performance, offering personalized feedback that aligns with each level of the Kirkpatrick model.
elements.sent-2411-082: For instance, LLMs can assess student reactions at Level 1 by analyzing feedback surveys and discussions to identify common challenges or misunderstandings.
elements.sent-2411-083: At Level 2, LLMs can evaluate knowledge acquisition by reviewing assignments and operations to offer tailored suggestions for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-084: For Levels 3 and 4, LLMs can analyze behavioral changes and long-term results by tracking student performance in applied projects or research, providing insights into how well they translate learning into practical outcomes.
elements.section-2411-004: Future Factory: Integration of SMC SiF-400 and Digital Twin Technology
elements.sent-2411-085: In the System and Security Laboratory (S\&SLab) at The University of Arizona, the concept of the ``Future Factory'' has been developed by integrating the SMC SiF-400 system with digital twin technology and generative AI, and the framework is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:FutureFactory .}
elements.sent-2411-086: The SMC SiF-400, a modular and highly automated intelligent factory training system \cite{sif400 , is augmented by digital twins designed with varying degrees of fidelity to enhance the educational process by aligning Digital Twin fidelity with learners' cognitive and skill development stages.}
elements.sent-2411-087: It equips learners with the necessary tools and knowledge to be successful in modern industrial environments by providing a scalable and interactive platform that evolves alongside the student's educational journey.
elements.sent-2411-088: In addition, we integrated a Generative AI-based tutor to evaluate the learning, and we used the Kirkpatrick model to assess students' learning status through a customized LLM.
elements.sent-2411-089: This framework ensures that students are well-prepared to meet the complexities of contemporary industrial roles, from basic operations to high-level research and innovation.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/Future_Factory.PNG} \caption{The Framework of S\&SLab's Future Factory with Multi-Fidelity Digital Twin Ecosystem} \label{fig:FutureFactory}
elements.sent-2411-090: The Future Factory employs a low-fidelity Digital Twin at the undergraduate level, introducing students to fundamental concepts in Industry 4.0 and intelligent manufacturing.
elements.sent-2411-091: This model simplifies the factory's processes, focusing on basic operations such as container filling and palletizing within a controlled environment.
elements.sent-2411-092: The objective at this stage is to facilitate the acquisition of foundational knowledge, aligning with Bloom's Taxonomy's emphasis on ``Remembering'' and ``Understanding.'' By interacting with these simplified systems, students develop a basic comprehension of industrial systems without being overwhelmed by the complexities inherent in higher-fidelity simulations.
elements.sent-2411-093: To achieve this educational goal, we use Photogrammetry to construct low-fidelity digital twins.
elements.sent-2411-094: This technology uses multiple photos to reconstruct physical objects into virtual three-dimensional models.
elements.sent-2411-095: Alhamadah et al. proposed that photogrammetry can effectively and accurately visualize physical processes\cite{alhamadah2024photogrammetry .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Using consumer devices like the iPhone 15 Pro, we can capture 3D contour data of industrial equipment and process these images through 3D scanning tools to Map 3D models of industrial equipment.
elements.sent-2411-097: The reliability of this technology lies in the error rate between the actual manually measured data and the final 3D model, which ultimately showed an overall average error of 4.97\%.
elements.sent-2411-098: This demonstrates that photogrammetry can accurately represent a virtual system, even using lower-cost consumer equipment, providing a way to construct a digital twin 3D model quickly.
elements.sent-2411-099: Figure \ref{fig:VR shows an example of using this technology to construct a digital twin in a VR scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-100: We built a virtual factory in Unity, imported the 3D model built using photogrammetry, and used Blender to create behavioral animations.
elements.sent-2411-101: Students can observe mechanical movements and behaviors by pressing the start button.
elements.sent-2411-102: Through this approach, students can gain a higher sense of engagement and hands-on experience interacting with simplified systems while avoiding the complexity of real-world systems or high-fidelity simulations.
elements.sent-2411-103: This teaching method can focus more on the learning goals designed by the teacher at a low cost and risk, enhance students' understanding of the concept of the target system, and promote their thinking and innovation capabilities in the context of Industry 4.0.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Medium-Fidelity Digital Twin for Master Student
elements.sent-2411-104: For master's students, the Future Factory advances to a medium-fidelity Digital Twin.
elements.sent-2411-105: This model integrates more complex systems within the SMC SiF-400, allowing students to engage in production planning, process optimization, and troubleshooting.
elements.sent-2411-106: The medium-fidelity environment aligns with the cognitive demands of ``Applying'' and ``Analyzing,'' as outlined in Bloom's Taxonomy.
elements.sent-2411-107: Here, students gain practical experience with tools such as the SIFMES-400 Management Execution Software, which enhances their ability to apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios, thus preparing them for roles in process engineering and management.
elements.sent-2411-108: To achieve this objective, we have constructed an Experiment VPN environment\cite{kolcu2020fctaas and deployed SCADA BR and OpenPLC, allowing students to connect securely and interact with SCADA BR and OpenPLC systems, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:VPN .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In this experimental setting, students can simulate real-world industrial automation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-110: The integration of these tools enables students to gain a deeper understanding of key control points and data collection processes within production workflows, thereby enhancing their skills in real-time monitoring, data analysis, and troubleshooting.
elements.sent-2411-111: This Experiment VPN also features interoperability with the SiF-400 system, enabling seamless integration of data and control functions, further broadening student engagement within this digital twin environment.
elements.sent-2411-112: Through this comprehensive hands-on experience, students not only simulate the operations of actual industrial control systems in a virtual environment but also learn how to apply cybersecurity techniques to protect control systems within critical infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-113: These skills are crucial for their future careers in process engineering, manufacturing management, and industrial automation.
elements.sent-2411-114: Moreover, the combination of SCADA BR and OpenPLC provides students with a cost-effective approach to field training, allowing them to experience the value and potential of data-driven application systems in digital transformation.
elements.sent-2411-115: We have designed multiple learning modules to strengthen further students' understanding of SCADA systems, including real-time data monitoring, anomaly simulation, and data report generation.
elements.sent-2411-116: These modules encourage students to engage in reflection and critical thinking during the learning process, enhancing their analytical and problem-solving abilities in alignment with Bloom’s Taxonomy levels of "Applying" and "Analyzing.".
elements.subsection-2411-005: High-Fidelity Digital Twin for Doctoral Student
elements.sent-2411-117: The Future Factory reaches its highest fidelity at the doctoral level, providing a detailed, real-time digital twining of the SMC SiF-400 system.
elements.sent-2411-118: This high-fidelity Digital Twin includes advanced features such as AI-driven analytics, real-time data integration, and cybersecurity protocols\cite{lin2023dt4i4 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: The high-fidelity model is tailored to support the cognitive processes of ``Evaluating'' and ``Creating,'' critical for doctoral research and innovation.
elements.sent-2411-120: Within this environment, doctoral students can design and test new industrial processes, develop custom APIs, and explore the full potential of Industry 4.0 technologies, thereby contributing to the advancement of intelligent manufacturing methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-121: To address these challenges, we leverage the power of generative AI.
elements.sent-2411-122: In the context of Industry 4.0, constructing flexible digital twin behaviors is a significant challenge, involving high training data costs and behavior changes over time.
elements.sent-2411-123: Recent advancements have shown that LLMs can effectively forecast time series data without extensive training\cite{gruver2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-124: By dynamically adjusting LLM inputs based on historical and real-time measurements, we can generate time series data that closely approximates the physical system’s behavior over time.
elements.sent-2411-125: This adaptive modeling capability allows the high-fidelity Digital Twin to continuously learn and refine its predictive algorithms, minimizing the need for large, costly datasets.
elements.sent-2411-126: In Figure \ref{fig:LLMTime , we implement a dynamic time series prediction method within the high-fidelity Digital Twin using a few-shot learning approach with LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-127: This enables real-time system adjustments based on evolving industrial processes.
elements.sent-2411-128: This approach enhances the flexibility of Digital Twin construction and aligns with the principles of Industry 4.0, where the ability to adapt to changing conditions and real-time data integration are crucial for intelligent manufacturing.
elements.sent-2411-129: Integrating a generative AI-based virtual tutor with the SMC SiF-400 system provides personalized, instant guidance based on learners’ needs.
elements.sent-2411-130: Using the design advantages of large-scale language models for solving generalized tasks, the customized AI virtual tutor can directly control the SMC SiF-400 machinery through API, adjust parameters, and execute instructions to create learning situations.
elements.sent-2411-131: Users can interact with the virtual tutor, ask questions, and operate the machine.
elements.sent-2411-132: The virtual tutor will continuously monitor the learner's input and the machine's response to evaluate performance, provide feedback, make suggestions for improvement, and make up for deficient knowledge.

elements.sent-2411-135: The virtual tutor can provide more accurate and particular guidance by establishing a database that includes various machine operation guides, troubleshooting methods, and frequently asked questions.

elements.sent-2411-142: This white paper explores the application of digital twin technology in industrial education and proposes a system framework that integrates different digital twin model fidelity with different educational stages.
elements.sent-2411-143: By mapping low-, medium-, and high-fidelity digital twins to varying stages of Bloom's cognitive domain, the training effectiveness is then evaluated through the Kirkpatrick Model.
elements.sent-2411-144: This study provides a systematic approach to help educators select appropriate teaching tools based on students' cognitive needs.
elements.sent-2411-145: This framework supports the memorization and understanding of essential knowledge at the undergraduate education stage to high-level analysis, evaluation, and innovation at the doctoral education stage.
elements.sent-2411-146: It can also improve students' learning effectiveness in industrial education.
elements.sent-2411-147: In addition, this white paper introduces the concept of ``Future Factory,'' which provides suitable industrial education scenarios for different education stages through the combination of SMC SiF-400 system and digital twin technology.
elements.sent-2411-148: Different fidelity digital twin designs of future factory designs can improve the effectiveness of industrial education and ensure students' flexibility and competitiveness in the coming industrial environment, thus promoting the innovation and development of modern industrial education.

elements.sent-2411-150: Artificial intelligence, especially with the emergence of generative AI (gen-AI), a subset of AI that focuses on creating new content, is driving a transformative revolution across all industries.
elements.sent-2411-151: This evolution opens new avenues for training and education, creating opportunities to upskill, adapt, and modify curriculum, coursework, and training to emerging demands, keeping pace with rapid technological changes while ensuring learning efficiency, scalability, and suitability for distance learning.
elements.sent-2411-152: This book chapter proposes PRISM: Personalized, Rapid, and Immersive Skill Mastery, a scalable framework that explores the use of gen-AI, to personalize experiential learning for each trainee's learning needs through Digital Twins and trainee sentiment analysis.
elements.sent-2411-153: This book chapter also introduces a Multi-Fidelity Digital Twin for Education (MFDT-E) framework, a scalable framework that maps Digital Twin design requirements to Bloom's Taxonomy, and the Kirkpatrick model to access learning outcomes at different levels.
elements.sent-2411-154: The MFDT-E framework quantifies digital twins' (DT) design requirements through their use of fidelity, classifying them broadly into three separate levels- low, medium, and high — corresponding to undergraduate, master's, and doctoral education stages.
elements.sent-2411-155: The MFDT-E, then integrated into the PRISM framework, allows personalized experiential learning, where the trainee performs experiential learning exercises through interaction with the DT, while the training is personalized through the use of Gen-AI-based sentiment analysis to measure student knowledge comprehension instep with Bloom's taxonomy, and usage of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to adapt and generate new content when student comprehension drops below the targets defined in the courses learning outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-156: This study demonstrates the effectiveness of the PRISM framework by combining generative AI and multi-fidelity digital twins.
elements.sent-2411-157: GPT-4 achieved a 91\% F1 score in zero-shot sentiment analysis of teacher-student dialogues, while GPT-3.5 reached 79.5\% accuracy in internet slang scenarios, outperforming traditional models.
elements.sent-2411-158: A qualitative-to-quantitative sentiment approach revealed emotional dynamics during learning, supporting personalized feedback.
elements.sent-2411-159: Four VR modules—Home Scene, Factory Floor Tour, Capping Station Digital Twin, and PPE Inspection Training—were developed to deliver immersive, low-fidelity Industry 4.0 training, highlighting the system's scalability and educational value.
elements.sent-2411-160: The growth of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has revolutionized the world, transforming all aspects of modern life, including all topics in electrical engineering, computer engineering, industrial engineering, systems engineering, and mechanical engineering.
elements.sent-2411-161: This transformation has not only changed the topics that need to be taught to the students but also changed the scale of teaching, with an ever-present need to modernize the curriculum to incorporate the rapidly evolving learning needs of the industry while ensuring compliance with traditional curriculum certification frameworks like ABET.
elements.sent-2411-162: One example of AI transforming learning and training needs is the rapid adoption of automated systems, like smart manufacturing plants and autonomous vehicles, which are changing today's engineering workforce needs, increasing skillset gaps, especially for the older workforce.
elements.sent-2411-163: With an increasing emphasis on STEM skillsets like robotics, automation, artificial intelligence (AI), and security, the workforce must be reskilled and upskilled to meet future industry needs while at the same time training the new workforce through realistic and hands-on experiential learning.
elements.sent-2411-164: However, such hands-on experiential learning requires access to specialized hardware that is not easily accessible, is expensive, has a huge learning curve, and has training limitations due to the risk of personnel or equipment damage.
elements.sent-2411-165: In addition to this, such workforce development programs have to train large groups of people globally from vastly different backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-166: Trainees/Students from such different backgrounds have different learning styles depending on their diverse upbringings, educational backgrounds, and motivations, all contributing to their successful program completion.
elements.sent-2411-167: Successful program completion is especially challenging for students from marginalized, underrepresented communities, as a historical lack of access to high-quality education during their formative years (pre/middle/high schools) impedes their success.
elements.sent-2411-168: To address these challenges, there is a pressing need for innovative solutions that provide scalable, safe, and personalized hands-on training environments.
elements.sent-2411-169: One promising approach is using virtual and augmented learning tools that simulate real-world engineering systems.
elements.sent-2411-170: Among these tools, Digital Twin (DT) technology is a transformative solution bridging the gap between physical hardware and digital learning environments.
elements.sent-2411-171: DTs were initially conceptualized in aerospace as a digital counterpart to physical systems, facilitates real-time interaction between virtual models and their physical counterparts through continuous status updates.
elements.sent-2411-172: Driven by the increasing demand for advanced monitoring, diagnostics, and predictive analytics in industrial systems and the advancements brought by Industry 4.0, DTs have emerged as a key enabler of complex real-time behavior modeling.
elements.sent-2411-173: By integrating data from diverse sensors and IoT devices, these DTs accurately reflect physical systems while enhancing predictive capabilities, enabling industrial system optimization, fault detection, and lifecycle management.
elements.sent-2411-174: Furthermore, integrating machine learning and artificial intelligence has significantly enhanced the adaptability of DTs, improving the accuracy of behavior models under varying conditions.
elements.sent-2411-175: As a result, their applications across manufacturing, aerospace, and energy sectors have improved efficiency, reliability, and cost-effectiveness \cite{soori2023digital .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Given these capabilities, DTs are increasingly being used in educational settings as immersive and interactive training platforms.
elements.sent-2411-177: When integrated into online courses, they allow learners to engage in realistic simulations of engineering systems—without needing physical access to expensive or hazardous equipment.
elements.sent-2411-178: This makes DTs particularly valuable for distance learning and remote workforce development, offering a safe, cost-effective, and scalable alternative to traditional lab-based instruction.
elements.sent-2411-179: Furthermore, by combining the flexibility of online education with the experiential nature of hands-on training, DT–based learning environments can improve engagement, motivation, and learning outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-180: This is especially beneficial for working professionals juggling jobs and family commitments, as well as students from underrepresented or under-resourced backgrounds who may lack access to physical labs or training facilities.

elements.sent-2411-182: Although DTs provides an effective and engaging simulation environment, the presence of a realistic training platform alone is not sufficient to ensure successful learning outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-183: Learners, especially those from non-traditional backgrounds, still require guidance, contextual understanding, and timely feedback to make the most of these experiences \cite{banks2019mitigating .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Traditionally, this role is filled by skilled instructors who can adapt their teaching to the individual needs of the learners.
elements.sent-2411-185: However, with the scale and diversity of online learners, providing such personalized instruction becomes a significant challenge \cite{jaggars2011online .}
elements.sent-2411-186: This is where Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a transformative opportunity.
elements.sent-2411-187: By leveraging recent advancements in natural language processing and AI, LLMs can serve as intelligent, always-available teaching assistants that help bridge the instructional gap in online and remote learning.

elements.sent-2411-191: Within PRISM, learners interact not only with a simulated engineering system via a DT, but also receive intelligent support through an embedded AI tutor, creating a comprehensive and self-guided learning ecosystem that aligns with the principles of being as helpful, honest, and harmless (HHH) as possible \cite{askell2021hhh .}
elements.sent-2411-192: The main contributions of this chapter are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-193: PRISM Framework: Proposed the PRISM (Personalized, Rapid, and Immersive Skill Mastery) framework, which leverages Generative AI and DT technology to enable personalized learning.

elements.sent-2411-203: In this section, we review three key areas relevant to our study: DTs, their applications in industrial education, and the role of large language models (LLMs) in educational settings.
elements.sent-2411-204: DT technology has evolved from its initial aerospace applications to become a critical tool in various industries, enabling real-time system monitoring, predictive maintenance, and operational optimization.

elements.section-2411-006: PRISM - Personalized Rapid and Immersive Skill Mastery
elements.sent-2411-253: In this section, we introduce the core concepts of the PRISM (Personalized Rapid and Immersive Skill Mastery) framework, which integrates multi-fidelity DTs with generative AI as a tutor to achieve rapid, personalized, and immersive skill development.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Multi-Fidelity Digital Twin for Education Framework
elements.sent-2411-254: The Multi-Fidelity Digital Twin for Education Framework (MFDT-E) is an educational framework grounded in the concept of Multi-Fidelity Digital Twins, which integrates multiple educational levels with Bloom's Taxonomy and the Kirkpatrick evaluation model (see Figure \ref{fig:mfdt-e ) \cite{lin2025personalized,krathwohl2002revision,smidt2009kirkpatrick .}}
elements.sent-2411-255: This framework interweaves these four components to form a comprehensive structure for both learning and assessment.
elements.sent-2411-256: By leveraging DT technology, it aims to fulfill industrial education objectives while guiding learners from foundational understanding to creative application, all while addressing measurable learning outcomes and behavioral change.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/DT_Edu.PNG} \caption{Educational Learning Stages and Outcome Mapping Based on a Multi-Fidelity Digital Twin Model} \label{fig:mfdt-e}
elements.sent-2411-257: In the educational needs of different stages, multi-fidelity DTs can become an educational tool, providing reasonable learning assistance for different educational levels and making educational goals at different stages successful.
elements.sent-2411-258: The integration of multi-fidelity digital twin (DT) technology into educational settings demonstrates strong alignment with established educational objectives, particularly those defined by Bloom’s taxonomy.
elements.sent-2411-259: At the foundational level, low-fidelity DTs—typically composed of simple three-dimensional models and basic animations—serve as effective tools for introductory education.
elements.sent-2411-260: These models assist learners in grasping fundamental concepts and cultivating initial interest in the subject matter.
elements.sent-2411-261: Such applications correspond to the lower tiers of Bloom’s taxonomy, namely Remember (retrieving relevant knowledge from long-term memory) and Understand (interpreting the meaning of instructional content, including oral, written, and graphical communication).
elements.sent-2411-262: In more advanced educational contexts, such as at the Master’s level, medium-fidelity DTs offer capabilities such as virtual commissioning, behavioral simulation, and rule-based automation.
elements.sent-2411-263: These afford learners the opportunity to engage with more complex scenarios, facilitating deeper cognitive engagement.
elements.sent-2411-264: These applications reflect the intermediate cognitive processes of Bloom’s taxonomy, particularly Apply (executing procedures in defined contexts) and Analyze (decomposing information into components and understanding their interrelationships within a structural framework).
elements.sent-2411-265: At the doctoral level, high-fidelity DTs incorporate advanced functionalities, including real-time system interaction, predictive analytics powered by machine learning, and multi-model interoperability.
elements.sent-2411-266: These features support higher-order cognitive activities such as creative problem-solving and critical evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-267: As such, they align with the highest levels of Bloom’s taxonomy—Evaluate (formulating judgments based on defined criteria and standards) and Create (assembling components to produce innovative and coherent outcomes).
elements.sent-2411-268: In evaluating students' learning effectiveness, we introduce the Kirkpatrick model \cite{smidt2009kirkpatrick into the framework, which can ensure a more comprehensive and and honest evaluation of students' learning effectiveness at different educational levels.}
elements.sent-2411-269: At the undergraduate level, using low-fidelity DTs, the emphasis is on the first level: reaction, focusing on how students perceive the training, its relevance to their future careers, and their participation in the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-270: As students advance to the Master's degree, Level 2: Learning becomes the focus, assessing how students acquire expected knowledge, skills, and attitudes through mid-fidelity DTs, thereby facilitating deeper application and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-271: At the doctoral level, using high-fidelity DTs, the framework moves to Level 3: Behaviors and Level 4: Result.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Low Fidelity DT for Undergraduate, Professional Certificate and K-12
elements.sent-2411-272: At the undergraduate level, educational emphasis is placed on building foundational knowledge and cultivating essential technical skills, equipping students with the basic competencies required for effective problem-solving \cite{koen1998abet .}
elements.sent-2411-273: In this context, low-fidelity DTs can offer simplified representations of industrial systems that facilitate conceptual understanding.
elements.sent-2411-274: While low-fidelity DTs cannot simulate precise system behavior, these models are adequate for tasks involving the core principles of ``remembering'' and ``understanding.'' Low-fidelity DTs can enable students to engage in fundamental aspects of system operation without being overwhelmed by the complexity of high-fidelity simulations, causing additional learning confusion \cite{russell1984effects .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Medium Fidelity DT for Master Degree
elements.sent-2411-275: As students advance to the postgraduate level, especially master's programs, the educational focus shifts to ``applied'' and ``analytical'' knowledge \cite{cranch1994next,backa2014future .}
elements.sent-2411-276: At the master's level, medium-fidelity DTs become more appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-277: These models offer a higher level of detail and interactivity, enabling students to solve complex problems and analyze real-world scenarios with greater precision \cite{choe2020master .}
elements.sent-2411-278: Medium-fidelity DTs are particularly suitable for project-based learning, by modeling realistic system behavior and incorporating rule-based logic or behavioral models, these DTs support the development of applied engineering skills and promote deeper engagement with domain-specific content.
elements.sent-2411-279: As a result, students reinforce their theoretical understanding and enhance their ability to address industry-relevant problems through iterative experimentation and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-280: The ability to manipulate variables, test hypotheses, and analyze results in a controlled digital environment is consistent with the cognitive processes of application and analysis described in Bloom's taxonomy.
elements.sent-2411-281: Medium-fidelity DTs can simulate manufacturing processes with detailed parameters that students can adjust to optimize output or resolve potential issues, promoting a deeper understanding of system dynamics and interdependencies.
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: High Fidelity DT for Doctoral Degree
elements.sent-2411-282: At the doctoral level, the educational focus is on ``creating'' and ``evaluating'' new knowledge, requiring the highest degree of cognitive engagement.
elements.sent-2411-283: High-fidelity DTs provide the detailed, accurate, and dynamic modeling needed for advanced research and innovation at the doctoral level.
elements.sent-2411-284: These models enable doctoral students to design and test new systems, conduct complex experiments, and evaluate the feasibility of novel solutions in virtual environments that closely reflect real-world conditions \cite{buswell2021purpose .}
elements.sent-2411-285: Integrating real-time data, advanced physics-based modeling, and artificial intelligence-driven analytics in high-fidelity DT supports the rigorous requirements of doctoral research, allowing for exploring cutting-edge technologies and developing new methods.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Leveraging Generative AI-based Technologies for DT-based Personalized Learning

elements.sent-2411-339: Sentiment analysis in informal online conversations presents unique challenges due to the widespread use of internet slang, abbreviations, and context-dependent expressions.
elements.sent-2411-340: To evaluate the performance of large language models (LLMs) in this domain, we conducted a zero-shot classification task using the TSATC dataset, which contains sentences featuring internet slang.
elements.sent-2411-341: Unlike traditional sentiment analysis tasks, where models are fine-tuned on labeled datasets, this evaluation aimed to assess how well pre-trained LLMs generalize to sentiment classification without prior exposure to the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-342: To ensure consistency in evaluation, we designed a structured prompt to instruct the model on sentiment classification rules.
elements.sent-2411-343: The definitions of positive and negative sentiment were explicitly provided, along with guidelines on handling user mentions (e.g., "@username") and expected response formatting.
elements.sent-2411-344: The prompt instructed the model to classify each sentence into a binary sentiment label (`1` for positive, `0` for negative) and return the results in an array format.
elements.sent-2411-345: This design ensured that the model adhered to a strict evaluation framework while reducing response variance.
elements.sent-2411-346: The following is the defined prompt for the evaluation of the TSATC dataset: \begin{formal You are an advanced sentiment analysis tool.}
elements.sent-2411-348: Please analyze the sentiment of sentences and follow the rules to return the result.
elements.sent-2411-349: Rule: - Positive Sentiment Definition: Happy, Excited.
elements.sent-2411-351: In Table~\ref{tab:llm-sentiment (a), we compare the performance of GPT-3.5 Turbo and Llama 2 (7B) on the TSATC test dataset using key evaluation metrics, including accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and $F_1$ score.}
elements.sent-2411-352: GPT-3.5 Turbo achieved an accuracy of 79.51\%, outperforming Llama 2 (75.79\%).
elements.sent-2411-353: In particular, GPT-3.5 exhibited higher precision (78.
elements.sent-2411-354: In contrast, Llama 2 demonstrated higher sensitivity (88.54\%), suggesting a stronger ability to identify positive sentiment, although at the cost of lower specificity (62.98\%).
elements.sent-2411-355: In this case, different LLM models offer varying trade-offs between specificity and sensitivity with ethical implications for student learning, in terms of prioritizing support for more successful students versus those who struggle with learning.
elements.sent-2411-356: In Table~\ref{tab:llm-sentiment (b), we compare LLM-based zero shot approaches with traditional neural network (NN) models, including GRU and CNN architectures trained on sentiment datasets of different sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-357: The results show that GPT-3.5 Turbo (79.51\%) and Llama 2 (75.79\%) achieved competitive performance against NN models trained with up to 10K labeled examples.
elements.sent-2411-358: While the best-performing NN model (GRU + CNN, 10K samples) reached an accuracy of 78.58\%, GPT-3.5 Turbo slightly outperformed it, highlighting the capability of modern LLMs to handle sentiment analysis tasks effectively without task-specific training.
elements.sent-2411-359: These findings suggest that large language models can generalize well to internet slang sentiment analysis in a zero-shot setting, reducing the dependency on large labeled datasets.

elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Qualitative to Quantitative Sentiment Analysis - Teacher-Student Conversations
elements.sent-2411-360: Traditional sentiment analysis often relies on classification (positive/ neutral/negative), which may not capture the nuanced emotional dynamics in teacher-student conversations.
elements.sent-2411-361: To address this limitation, we designed a LLM-based approach that enables a qualitative to quantitative transformation of sentiment, using a zero-shot strategy to evaluate sentiment intensity in a structured manner.
elements.sent-2411-362: You are doing a qualitative-to-quantitative task for sentiment analysis.
elements.sent-2411-363: I will provide a batch of sentences to you, and you need to follow these rules for sentiment analysis: - Do not provide an explanation.
elements.sent-2411-364: The following is the conversation; please analyze it: \end{formal We selected a teacher-student conversation from the EduTalk Sentiment Dataset as a case study to demonstrate how large language models (LLMs) can perform qualitative-to-quantitative sentiment evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-365: The selected conversation, presented in Table \ref{tab:TS_Conv_Case , captures an educational dialogue where a teacher attempts to explain atomic concepts to a student, who exhibits varying degrees of engagement and frustration throughout the interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-366: We employed GPT-4 with a temperature setting of 0.2 to analyze sentiment dynamics, leveraging the structured prompt engineering outlined in Appendix 2.
elements.sent-2411-367: Given that LLM-generated responses can exhibit variability, we adopted a Monte Carlo-style approach, running the same conversation through GPT-4 $n$ times ($n=20$) to derive robust statistical measurements of sentiment shifts \cite{katz2024gpt .}

elements.subsection-2411-012: Enhancing LLM Expertise with GraphRAG: A Cybersecurity Education Use Case
elements.sent-2411-372: Figure~\ref{fig:graph illustrates a subset of a knowledge graph for packet sniffing topics generated using GraphRAG \cite{edge2024local .}}
elements.sent-2411-373: GraphRAG is an advanced implementation of the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) paradigm, which enhances the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) by integrating them with external knowledge sources.
elements.sent-2411-374: Unlike traditional LLMs that rely solely on pre-trained parameters, RAG enables dynamic access to relevant documents or structured knowledge bases during inference.
elements.sent-2411-375: This mechanism allows the model to retrieve contextually appropriate information and incorporate it into generated responses, thus improving factual accuracy and domain relevance \cite{lewis2020retrieval .}
elements.sent-2411-376: In the context of GraphRAG, the system utilizes knowledge graphs as the retrieval backbone.
elements.sent-2411-377: These graphs organize domain knowledge into structured nodes and edges, capturing the relationships between concepts, tools, and techniques—such as those related to packet sniffing.
elements.sent-2411-378: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:graph , the knowledge graph aggregates information from diverse sources to form a coherent and comprehensive representation of cybersecurity concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-379: By leveraging GraphRAG’s graph-based retrieval mechanism, the LLM can generate more accurate, contextualized, and explainable responses.
elements.sent-2411-380: This is particularly valuable in educational settings, where precision and clarity are essential.
elements.sent-2411-381: Unlike static fine-tuning approaches, GraphRAG allows for on-the-fly knowledge updates without retraining the model, making it highly adaptable to evolving domains like cybersecurity.
elements.sent-2411-382: This approach not only improves the model’s reasoning over domain-specific knowledge but also significantly reduces the computational cost of keeping educational content up-to-date.

elements.sent-2411-383: The DT labs aims to provide a 4IR training environment by integrating the photogrammetry method proposed by Ahmed et al. \cite{alhamadah2024photogrammetry and the DDD-GenDT method proposed by Lin et al. \cite{lin2024ddd , leveraging a Virtual Reality (VR) environment with a microservice architecture to deliver virtual equipment and their behaviors to users.}}
elements.sent-2411-384: VR enables an immersive training experience using specialized 4IR hardware, allowing students to safely experiment with complex industrial scenarios, including cybersecurity threats and catastrophic system failures, without real-world harmful risks to human or equipment safety \cite{hamilton2021immersive,carruth2017virtual .}
elements.sent-2411-385: For example, in 4IR cybersecurity training \cite{satam2020wids,tunc2015claas , students can be virtually transported to a manufacturing factory floor, where they can perform and observe the effects of data injection attacks on industrial robots \cite{satam2023cps,satam2020wids,shao2021multi , allowing for meaningful, risk-free learning experiences.}}
elements.sent-2411-386: The Unity-based DT labs provides an immersive, interactive, and gamified learning experience, with an initial version featuring four modules that utilize low-fidelity DTs to introduce fundamental Industry 4.0 concepts and skill development.
elements.sent-2411-387: These modules allow students to explore realistic manufacturing scenarios, interact with virtual components, and enhance decision-making skills, fostering a deeper understanding of Industry 4.0 technologies in an engaging, adaptive, and personalized environment.

elements.sent-2411-388: Here is a brief description of each module and its main function:.
elements.sent-2411-389: Module 1 - Home Scene: The Home Scene, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:Learning_Interface (a), serves as the initial training environment for students.}
elements.sent-2411-390: This module provides an introduction to controller usage and the Learning Interface UI.
elements.sent-2411-391: The Learning Interface UI in the Home Scene includes instructional slides that offer a step-by-step guide on how to navigate and utilize the PRISM platform effectively.
elements.sent-2411-392: Module 2 - Factory Floor Tour: The Factory Floor Tour, shown in Figure \ref{fig:Learning_Interface (b), allows students to explore a smart manufacturing environment.}
elements.sent-2411-393: In this module, students participate in a guided tour of a realistic Industry 4.0 factory floor, introducing them to key components and operational aspects of such an environment.
elements.sent-2411-394: Students can interact with information panels by clicking on the `Pink boxes' to access additional details.
elements.sent-2411-395: Module 3 - Capping Station Tour: The Capping Station Tour, depicted in Figure \ref{fig:Learning_Interface (c), presents an interactive experience featuring the operational DT of the Capping Station \cite{alhamadah2024photogrammetry , a part of the UArizona Future Factory.}}
elements.sent-2411-396: This model, developed using photogrammetry techniques, enables students to explore different components of the Capping Station and operate its DT in VR, allowing them to observe its functions in real time.
elements.sent-2411-397: Module 4 - Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Inspection Training: The PPE Inspection Training module, as seen in Figure \ref{fig:Learning_Interface (d), reinforces proper Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) usage.}
elements.sent-2411-398: Students interact with virtual workers in an industrial setting to identify and correct PPE-related issues.
elements.sent-2411-399: Through various workplace scenarios, students ensure compliance with safety regulations and improve decision-making skills using interactive elements that simulate real-world environments.
elements.sent-2411-400: This chapter introduced the PRISM framework, Personalized, Rapid, and Immersive Skill Mastery, as a transformative educational paradigm that leverages generative AI, multi-fidelity DTs, and emotion-aware guidance to deliver scalable, personalized experiential learning.
elements.sent-2411-401: By integrating Bloom's taxonomy and the Kirkpatrick evaluation model, PRISM enables a structured, adaptive, and measurable pathway for technical education at undergraduate, master's, and doctoral levels.
elements.sent-2411-402: Using zero-shot sentiment analysis and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), the framework dynamically responds to learners’ cognitive and emotional states, offering real-time feedback and adaptive content generation.
elements.sent-2411-403: The high performance of GPT-4 and GPT-3.5 in various sentiment analysis tasks, including informal language scenarios, underscores the feasibility of deploying large language models as intelligent virtual tutors.
elements.sent-2411-404: Additionally, the development of immersive VR training modules demonstrates the scalability and accessibility of low-fidelity DTs in the foundational education of Industry 4.0.
elements.sent-2411-405: In summary, the PRISM framework offers a structured and adaptable approach to experiential learning, especially in settings where traditional training is limited by cost, accessibility, or safety constraints.
elements.sent-2411-406: Moreover, it supports the upskilling of the current workforce and empowers the next generation of engineers and technologists to thrive in a rapidly changing industrial landscape.
